key_insight: A frontier language model, constrained to explicit reaction-network reasoning, can generate experimentally testable catalyst-selectivity hypotheses and uncover control levers for CO2 electroreduction.
engineering_relevance: The framework enabled the design of a copper-iron oxide catalyst that achieved about 3x higher acetate selectivity than matched Cu-rich baselines, showing a practical path from mechanism to materials optimization.
method: Enforced network-invariant reasoning over explicit chemical reaction graphs instead of relying on static descriptors.
Used human-AI co-thinking to extract testable hypotheses about pathway competition in CO2 electroreduction.
Translated mechanistic levers into synthesis targets: local alkalinity, iron incorporation, and limited proton-donor access.
evidence_signal: The abstract reports prospective synthesis and experimental confirmation of a Cu-Fe oxide catalyst with roughly threefold improved acetate selectivity over Cu-rich controls.
watch_item: Performance depends on having accurate, explicit reaction networks and may not transfer cleanly when the mechanistic graph is incomplete or uncertain.
paper_title: Reaction-network reasoning with frontier models for experimentally confirmed catalyst-selectivity hypotheses
